what_it_does: Documents a measured performance analysis of the corpus_rerank cross-encoder path, identifies the CPU-bound root cause, lists loadout options that require a named decision-maker, and flags a data-shape side finding.
observations: The file is superseded as a tracker but kept as detailed measurement/analysis referenced by a board item and a decision card.
Stage B, the jina-reranker-v3 cross-encoder POST on CPU, dominates latency at roughly 1 second per candidate.
CPU-only serving is deliberate via CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" in ops/serve_rerank.sh, so slowness is a loadout cost, not a regression.
Four options are presented: keep rerank off hot paths, move jina-v3 to GPU, add a lighter CPU reranker tier, or shrink/background rerank candidate sets.
The CC consult path also pays the same CPU rerank cost.
A side finding notes that 13 of 24 pooled candidates had non-string output, causing rerank_hits to skip them silently in cross-space rerank.
standouts: The latency is per-candidate CPU forward-pass time, not warmup or passage length.
CPU-only rerank is an intentional loadout choice, making the fix a loadout decision rather than a code bug fix.
The real lever is whether to move jina-v3 to GPU, restrict rerank scope, or add a lighter tier.
Cross-space rerank silently excludes candidates whose output is not a string.
conventions: Uses decision-surface identifiers such as board://, decision://, and GAP-* references.
Separates measurement/analysis from tracking and decision authority.
Presents engineering options as requiring consult-before-deciding rather than unilateral changes.
Includes measured timings, candidate counts, and passage lengths.
concerns: Cross-space rerank silently skips non-string outputs, potentially underrepresenting relevant candidates.
The note defers all action to a named decision-maker without proposing a default.
Two distinct concerns, performance loadout and output-shape normalization, share the file even though the shape issue is noted as moved to a separate gap-note.
notes: A measured post-mortem of CPU-bound rerank latency that parks the fix behind a Tim loadout decision and flags a silent candidate-skipping data-shape issue.
questions: What is the available GPU VRAM headroom and current loadout that option 2 must fit into?
Which exact paths still invoke rerank and what are their latency budgets?
Has the non-string output shape been normalized since the side finding was moved to GAP-corpus-output-nonstring.md?
What do the referenced board item and decision card currently say?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to give the root cause and measured options for slow rerank; the file does that and also flags a data-shape side finding noted as moved to a separate gap-note. It matches its purpose.
apparent_themes: Decision authority separation between measurement and decision-making
Loadout tradeoffs among latency, VRAM, and ranking quality
Documenting the cost of an intentional choice
Data-shape hygiene and honest skipping behavior
intention_signals: It is trying to preserve a neutral, detailed performance analysis after the tracker moved elsewhere, and to push the real resolution to the named decision-maker rather than disguising a loadout choice as a code fix.
novelty: It explicitly treats CPU-only rerank slowness as a deliberate loadout cost rather than a bug, and uses short-passage measurements to rule out warmup and length-based explanations.
contribution: A measured, decision-ready account of rerank latency with enumerated options and a flagged data-shape caveat, useful for loadout planning and for understanding why rerank was removed from hot paths.
summary_for_embedding: A performance analysis note measuring jina-reranker-v3 CPU rerank at roughly one second per candidate, explaining that CPU-only serving is intentional, listing loadout options for Tim/lead, and flagging that cross-space rerank silently skips candidates with non-string output.
intention_for_embedding: To document the root cause and measured options for slow corpus reranking so the loadout decision can be made explicitly rather than treated as a code regression.
